stories.paragraph: In October 1905 a pogrom swept through the Jewish quarter of Odessa. The Rosenfeld family fled their tailor's shop by lamplight, father Yaakov carrying a bundle of prayer books, mother Chana carrying the youngest child, Daniel. Smoke rose from a neighbor's burning house as Cossacks rode past the end of the street.

The family boarded a steamer at Hamburg bound for Buenos Aires in the spring of 1906. They stood among hundreds on the third-class deck, wrapped in dark coats, watching the European coast fall away. A folded letter from the Jewish Colonization Association lay in Yaakov's inner pocket, promising farmland on the Argentine pampa.

In the Jewish agricultural colony of Moisés Ville on the open pampa, Yaakov walked between long rows of wheat with a scythe over his shoulder. The younger sons followed, gathering sheaves; in the distance, a new adobe synagogue rose from the flat horizon. A gaucho on horseback watched from a dirt road.

Inside the adobe synagogue on a Shabbat evening, Rabbi Mendel Rosenfeld — Yaakov's brother — chanted from a Torah scroll before a small congregation. Kerosene lamps lit the whitewashed walls. Women sat in a back row wrapped in dark shawls; a small boy leaned against his grandfather's knee.

The older Rosenfeld children made their lives across Argentina. Rivka stood behind the counter of a Yiddish bookshop on Calle Corrientes in Buenos Aires. Shmuel worked at a dairy cooperative in Entre Ríos. Leah, in a simple dress, spoke at a garment workers' meeting in a union hall, her hand raised mid-sentence.

Grown now, the youngest son Daniel rode south into Patagonia as a wool trader, a pack mule following his horse. He wore a sheepskin coat against the cold, a leather satchel of ledgers at his saddle. The snow-capped Andes rose behind him as he approached a distant trading post.

At a Mapuche trading post near Lago Nahuel Huapi, Daniel met Millaray Curiqueo, daughter of a Mapuche machi. She stood beside rolls of dyed wool and a hand-carved loom, wearing a silver trarilonko headband. He had come to buy her family's wool; he stayed to share mate and conversation.

In 1932 Daniel and Millaray married at the edge of the lake near Bariloche under a chuppah of four wooden poles strung with a prayer shawl. A Mapuche kultrun drum sounded as the rabbi and the machi stood side by side. Guests from both families formed a wide semicircle, snow on the distant peaks.

In a log house above the lake, Daniel and Millaray sat at a long table with three children. A Yiddish newspaper lay beside a Spanish primer and a Mapudungun songbook. Millaray ladled stew from a clay pot while Daniel cut bread; the eldest child read aloud in Spanish.

In the 1950s the family ran a general store on the main street of Bariloche, its painted sign reading ALMACÉN ROSENFELD Y CURIQUEO. Daniel, gray-bearded, stood at the counter weighing yerba mate on a brass scale while Millaray stocked shelves with wool blankets and canned goods. Their children unloaded a delivery wagon outside.

In 1977 the family gathered silently around a kitchen table, a photograph of their granddaughter Ester placed upright before them. She had been a university student in La Plata; military officers had taken her in the night. Millaray, in her seventies, held Daniel's hand; a candle burned beside the photograph.

Decades later, great-grandchildren held a Passover seder on the shore of Lago Nahuel Huapi. A long table covered in white linen held matzah, maror, and a kultrun drum laid at one end in memory. Mapuche elders and Rosenfeld cousins sat together; a child read the Four Questions in Spanish under the open sky.
